content.text: A Cowork
Project

is a named container that bundles related tasks together with three shared resources: a persistent workspace, a memory block, and a set of connector authorizations. Setting up a project correctly before you write the first task determines how smoothly multi-step work flows and how little duplication occurs across runs.
What a Project Contains

attrs.alt: Anatomy of a Cowork Project: a named container holding a persistent workspace, project memory, connected data sources, and an ordered list of tasks
attrs.title: Anatomy of a Cowork Project

content.text: The four components of a Cowork project work together: the
workspace
stores files that persist across all tasks;
memory
injects standing context at the start of every task;
connectors
are authenticated once and available to every task in the project; and
tasks
read from and write to the shared workspace, building on each other's outputs.
Creating a New Project
Projects are created from the Cowork home screen. The creation form asks for a name, an optional description, and an optional team. Three decisions at creation time shape everything that follows.
Decision 1: Project name and scope

The project name is both a label and a scope signal. A narrow, time-bounded name keeps the project focused and makes it easier to decide which tasks belong to it. A vague name leads to scope creep — tasks accumulate that share a workspace but have no meaningful relationship.

# Good project names (specific, bounded):
Q4 2025 Finance Audit
ACME Competitive Analysis -- Q1 2026
Engineering Hiring Pipeline -- H1 2026
Customer Churn Analysis -- December 2025

# Poor project names (too vague):
Finance stuff
Research
My project
Analysis
Decision 2: When to use a project vs a one-off task
Use a project when
Use a one-off task when
Multiple tasks share the same source data
The work is a single self-contained task with no follow-on
Each task's output is input to the next task
You do not need persistent memory or shared files
Recurring tasks run on the same dataset on a schedule
The task is exploratory and you do not know if there will be a second step
The same connector auth and standing context apply across tasks
The task uses a different data source from anything else in your queue
Decision 3: Team vs personal project

A personal project is visible only to you. A team project is visible to all workspace members you add, and they can view task history and submitted goals. Team projects are appropriate when multiple people submit tasks against the same dataset, or when a manager needs visibility into what the engine is doing. All workspace files are shared across team members regardless of who submitted the task.
Writing the Project Memory

Project memory is a plain text block that Cowork automatically prepends to every task in the project. Think of it as standing instructions that you would otherwise repeat in every goal statement. Good memory blocks cover four categories: output format preferences, domain conventions, audience definition, and source data context.

# Example project memory for a Finance reporting project:
Output format: markdown with ATX headings (##, ###)
Currency: USD; always show 2 decimal places
Numbers > 1000: format with comma separator (e.g. 1,204,500)
Audience: Finance leadership -- avoid jargon; spell out acronyms first use
Report tone: factual, concise; no hedging language
Source data: Q4 2025 budget system export
Fiscal year: Oct 2025 - Sep 2026

Memory is injected at the start of every task, so anything you put there is treated as a constraint on every goal in the project. Keep it accurate: outdated or contradictory memory confuses the engine. If memory applies only to certain tasks, put it in the individual task goal instead.
What to put in memory vs in the task goal
Put in project memory
Put in the task goal
Output format (markdown, CSV, JSON)
Specific file to read for this task
Audience and tone that applies to all outputs
Exact output path and filename
Domain conventions (currency format, date format)
Stopping condition (top 10, all rows where X)
Definitions (what counts as an overrun, what fiscal year covers)
Task-specific constraints that differ from the memory defaults
Connecting Data Sources

Connectors are authorized at the project level. Once a connector is added to a project, every task in that project can query it without re-authenticating. The authorization grants the engine read access to the data sources you specify — it does not grant write access unless you explicitly enable that for the connector.
Connector setup steps
In the project settings, open the Connections tab.
Click Add Connection and select the connector type (Drive, Slack, GitHub, Jira, etc.).
Complete the OAuth flow for that service. Cowork requests the minimum scopes needed for read access.

Scope the connector: for Drive, select the specific folder; for Slack, select the channels; for Jira, select the project key. Scoping limits what the engine can reach and reduces the risk of the engine querying unintended data.
Optionally, add the connector name and scope to the project memory so task goals can reference it without ambiguity.
Connector scope vs task scope

Connector scope at the project level sets the outer boundary of what the engine can reach. Task goal scope narrows further within that boundary. A Slack connector scoped to five channels at the project level does not mean every task will query all five — the task goal still needs to name which channels to search. The project connector only establishes that those channels are
accessible
Organizing the Workspace for Multi-Task Projects

The workspace is a flat directory by default. For projects with more than three or four tasks, imposing a folder structure from the first task prevents naming collisions and makes it clear which files are source data, which are intermediate, and which are final deliverables.

# Suggested /workspace/ layout for a multi-task project:
workspace/
  source/          # original uploaded files; never overwritten by tasks
    q4-budget.csv
    headcount.xlsx
  processed/       # outputs from ingestion and transformation tasks
    budget-clean.csv
    headcount-clean.csv
  analysis/        # outputs from analysis tasks
    cost-overruns.md
    headcount-gaps.md
  reports/         # final deliverables
    exec-summary.md
    board-deck-draft.md
  task-log.txt     # running log of what each task did
The most important rule: never let tasks overwrite source files. Put original uploads in a
source/
subfolder and instruct every task to write outputs elsewhere. Once a source file is overwritten in /workspace/, it is gone — there is no version history inside the workspace.

Upload source files once, into source/. Every task goal should reference them by read-only path and write to processed/, analysis/, or reports/. This pattern is the single biggest prevention against workspace corruption in multi-task projects.
Project Settings Reference
Setting
What it controls
Recommendation
Project name
Display label and scope signal for tasks
Specific and time-bounded; rename when scope changes
Project memory
Text injected at start of every task
Keep it accurate; review after completing each stage
Connectors
OAuth authorizations for external data sources
Scope to the minimum necessary folders, channels, or projects
Team members
Who can view and submit tasks to the project
Add only collaborators who need to see the workspace files
Workspace
The /workspace/ directory shared by all tasks
Establish a folder structure in the first task; protect source/ from writes
Common Setup Mistakes
Mistake
Symptom
Fix
Stale memory
Tasks use outdated conventions or reference the wrong fiscal year
Review memory before each new stage; update dates and definitions
Overly broad connector scope
Tasks search entire Drive or all Slack channels, producing irrelevant results
Narrow connector scope to specific folder or channels during setup
No workspace structure
Tasks overwrite each other's output; source files get corrupted by later tasks
First task creates source/, processed/, analysis/, reports/ subfolders
One project for all work
Workspace fills with unrelated files; memory applies to tasks where it does not belong
Create a new project for each distinct domain or time period
Uploading Source Files and Initializing the Workspace

Files reach the workspace through two paths: direct upload via the project UI, or by having the first task write them from a connected data source. The upload path is simpler for files you already have locally. The connector path is better for large files stored in Drive or data you want to pull fresh.
Direct upload
Open the project workspace tab and click Upload Files.
Files upload to the root of /workspace/ by default. After uploading, use a setup task to move them into the source/ subfolder.

Supported file types include CSV, Excel, PDF, plain text, markdown, and JSON. Binary formats like images and audio are not processed by the engine unless the task explicitly calls a vision or audio tool.
For large files (>50 MB), prefer the connector path — upload size limits apply to the UI uploader.
Using a setup task to initialize the workspace

For projects with more than a few tasks, the first task should create the folder structure and a task log rather than doing actual analysis. This costs one task run but saves confusion in every subsequent task.

# First task goal for a new project:
Set up the workspace structure for this project.

1. Create the following subdirectories in /workspace/:
   /workspace/source/
   /workspace/processed/
   /workspace/analysis/
   /workspace/reports/

2. Write a file /workspace/task-log.txt with the following content:
   Project: Q4 Finance Analysis
   Created: [today's date]
   Tasks will append a line here when they complete.

Do not read any external files. This is a setup task only.

After this setup task completes, every subsequent task knows the workspace layout and can reference paths confidently. The task log gives you a running record of what each task produced, which is useful when you return to a project after several days.
Scheduled Tasks Within a Project

Projects support scheduled tasks — goals that run automatically on a cron-like schedule without you submitting them manually. Scheduled tasks are useful for recurring data pulls, weekly digests, and regular monitoring tasks. They inherit the project memory and connector authorizations just like manually-submitted tasks.

# Example: Weekly Slack digest project with a scheduled task

Project name: Weekly Finance Digest
Project memory:
  Output: Slack message formatted with bullet points, no markdown headers
  Channel: #finance-digest
  Data source: Jira project FIN, Drive /Finance/Weekly/

Scheduled task (runs every Monday 07:00 UTC):
  Goal: Read all Jira tickets closed in the past 7 days in project FIN.
        Read the weekly summary file in Drive /Finance/Weekly/ for this week.
        Produce a bullet-point Slack digest:
          - 3 highlights from closed Jira tickets
          - 2 key numbers from the weekly summary
        Save the draft as /workspace/digest-draft.txt.
        Do not post to Slack -- I will review the draft first.
Scheduled task design rules
Always write to a draft file, not a live destination.
A scheduled task that posts directly to Slack or Jira without a review step can send wrong output before you notice. Write the draft to /workspace/ and review before the send step.
Include a date boundary in the goal.
Scheduled tasks run against whatever is currently in the data source. Without a date filter, a Monday digest task might pull all Jira tickets ever, not just those from the past week.
Check the step log after the first few runs.

Scheduled tasks run unattended; the step log is your only window into whether they are using the right data and producing expected output. Review the first three scheduled runs before trusting the output.
Project Lifecycle: Active, Paused, and Archived

Projects move through three states as work progresses. Understanding when to transition between them prevents workspace clutter and keeps the connector authorizations and memory blocks from becoming stale.
State
Description
When to use
Active
Tasks can be submitted; scheduled tasks run; workspace is writable
Work is in progress; at least one task per week
Paused
Scheduled tasks suspended; manual tasks still allowed; workspace readable
Work is temporarily halted but will resume; no need to archive yet
Archived
Read-only; no new tasks; workspace files preserved; connector auths remain but inactive
Work is complete; keep the project for reference but prevent accidental additions
Before archiving: workspace cleanup task

# Before archiving a project, run a cleanup task:
Review all files in /workspace/.
Move any file larger than 10 MB to /workspace/archive/.
List all files in /workspace/ with their sizes.
Write the list to /workspace/workspace-index.txt.

# After archiving:
# The project is read-only; workspace files are preserved
# but no new tasks can be submitted.
# Archived projects can be unarchived if needed.
Running a cleanup task before archiving keeps the workspace auditable: the workspace-index.txt file tells any future reviewer exactly what files were present and their sizes at close-out time.
Linking Projects: When One Project Reads Another's Output

Projects are isolated by design — one project cannot directly read another's /workspace/ directory. To pass data from one project to another, export it first: have a task in Project A write the output to a connected Drive folder, then have a task in Project B read it from that Drive folder. This makes the handoff explicit and auditable.
Handoff method
How it works
When to use
Drive export/import
Project A writes to Drive; Project B reads from same Drive folder
Both projects have Drive connector; standard for most use cases
Manual download/re-upload
Download file from Project A workspace in UI; upload to Project B
Sensitive data that should not pass through Drive; one-off handoffs
Evolving the Project Memory Over Time

Project memory is not static. It should evolve as you learn more about the data, make decisions about conventions, and complete stages of the work. A good practice is to version the memory by appending a comment at the top noting when and why it was updated.

# Project memory: version 1 (project start)
Output format: markdown
Currency: USD, 2 decimal places
Audience: Finance leadership
Source: Q4 2025 budget export

# Project memory: version 2 (after stage 1 complete)
Output format: markdown
Currency: USD, 2 decimal places
Audience: Finance leadership
Source: Q4 2025 budget export
Processed data: /workspace/processed/budget-clean.csv (1,204 rows, validated)
Cost overrun threshold: any variance > 5% or > $50,000 absolute
  -- defined after reviewing findings from Task 2

# Project memory: version 3 (approaching close-out)
# [same as v2 plus:]
Final report path: /workspace/reports/exec-summary.md
Board deck path: /workspace/reports/board-deck-draft.md
Do not create new analysis files; only update existing reports.

Notice that version 2 adds a concrete definition of cost overrun threshold — a convention that was decided after reviewing the data, not before. Putting it in memory means every subsequent task uses the same threshold without you repeating it in every goal. Version 3 adds the final report paths and a constraint that prevents tasks from creating new files, which protects the deliverables from being accidentally duplicated or overwritten.
Memory update triggers
After completing a stage:
add the processed file paths and any domain conventions discovered during that stage.
When you change a decision:
if you decide the report audience changed or the currency format should be different, update memory immediately so the change propagates to all future tasks.
Before close-out:
add a constraint to prevent tasks from writing to finalized paths, protecting the deliverables.
When data changes:
if source files are updated or replaced, update the memory to reflect the new file names and row counts.
Team Project Collaboration Patterns

In a team project, multiple people can submit tasks against the same workspace. This is powerful but requires coordination: two tasks running simultaneously against the same workspace can produce conflicting output if they write to the same file. Cowork does not lock files between tasks.
Pattern
How it works
Risk to avoid
Sequential ownership
Only one person submits tasks at a time; handoff is explicit (notify team before starting)
Low risk; recommended for most team projects
Partitioned workspace
Each team member writes to a named subfolder (e.g. analysis/alice/, analysis/bob/)
No file conflicts even when tasks run in parallel
Read-only shared base
All tasks read from source/ and processed/; only the project owner writes to reports/
Prevent overwriting shared outputs; enforce via memory constraint
Summary

A well-set-up Cowork project has a bounded name, accurate memory covering format and domain conventions, connectors scoped to the minimum necessary data sources, and a workspace directory structure that separates source files from outputs. Investing three to five minutes in setup before writing the first task eliminates the most common failure modes in multi-step work: stale context, overwritten source files, and connectors that reach too broadly. When the project scope changes, update the name and memory before starting the next stage.
